description: A self-contained project-fact corpus for mnemo's REAL job: precise project-fact recall + safe abstention. A fictional backend service 'orchard' (job scheduling) + a 'console' web UI + global engineering rules, with a question set across three slices: answerable (the fact is in the bank), irrelevant (nothing answers it -> REFUSE; on-topic traps), and superseded (a newer version exists -> gold is the CURRENT value). Gold is referenced by a stable memory 'key', never a volatile store id. Memories are ingested in array order, so a topic_key reused later supersedes the earlier one, and a newer coexisting note is more recent.

memories.content: Use Redis Streams (not RabbitMQ or SQS) for the job queue: at-least-once delivery with consumer groups, and we already operate Redis for caching, so it's one fewer system to run.

Queue backend bench at 10k jobs/s: Redis Streams p99 enqueue 1.2ms, RabbitMQ 4ms, SQS 18ms. Redis won on latency and ops simplicity; SQS's visibility-timeout model added complexity for no latency gain.
Jobs are idempotent: a dedup key = sha256(job_type + canonical payload) is checked against a Redis sorted-set scored by enqueue time; a re-enqueue of the same key within 24h is dropped.

Redis OOM incident: the dedup set used per-key TTLs but the set itself never shrank, so it grew unbounded. Fix: a sorted-set scored by timestamp plus a periodic ZREMRANGEBYSCORE sweep of entries older than 24h.
Failed jobs retry with exponential backoff (base 2s, factor 2, cap 5min, max 6 attempts), then move to a dead-letter stream for manual replay.
Retry jitter used each worker's local clock, so clock skew across workers bunched retries into thundering herds. Fix: jitter off the job's stored enqueue timestamp, not wall-clock now().
API auth is via static API keys sent in the Authorization header and checked against the api_keys Postgres table.
API auth uses short-lived JWTs (RS256, 15-minute expiry) issued by the auth service; the old static API keys are deprecated and removed.
Job rows live in Postgres (the jobs table); payloads over 256KB go to S3 and the row stores the S3 key, while smaller payloads inline in a JSONB column.
Delayed jobs are run with one timer per job (a scheduled task per delayed job that fires at run-at).
Delayed jobs use a Redis sorted-set scored by run-at; one mover process polls due entries every second and pushes them onto the ready stream, so there are no per-job timers.
Rate limiting on the enqueue endpoint is still a TODO — there are currently no per-caller limits.
The enqueue endpoint is rate-limited per API key with a Redis token bucket: 1000 requests/min, burst 2000; over-limit returns 429 with a Retry-After header.
Dead-letter replay: the API (POST /dlq/{id}/replay) is done; the console view to browse and replay dead-lettered jobs is still pending.
Tracing is OpenTelemetry into Tempo; each job carries a trace context propagated from the enqueue request, so a job's spans link back to the caller that submitted it.
Job workers exhausted the Postgres connection pool under load because each worker opened its own pool. Fix: a shared pgbouncer in transaction-pooling mode plus a per-worker pool cap of 5.
The enqueue API rejects payloads over 1MB with 413; 256KB-1MB go to S3, under 256KB inline. The 1MB ceiling keeps a single job from blocking a worker on a large download.
Workers autoscale on queue depth (KEDA on the Redis Streams lag metric), not CPU; target lag under 500 jobs, min 2 and max 50 replicas.

A poison job (always-crashing payload) retried 6 times then dead-lettered, but its retries starved healthy jobs in the same consumer group. Fix: a circuit-breaker that dead-letters immediately on a deterministic, non-retryable error class.
The REST API is versioned by URL prefix (/v1, /v2); a version is supported for 12 months after its successor ships, then sunset with a 410 Gone.
Liveness means the process is up; readiness means Redis and Postgres are reachable and the worker has claimed its consumer-group slot. Kubernetes routes and scales only on readiness.
The console is a React SPA built with Vite, talking to orchard over the public REST API; there is no server-side rendering — it ships as static assets behind a CDN.
The console authenticates operators via OIDC (Okta); the SPA keeps a short-lived access token in memory only (never localStorage) and refreshes it silently.
Server state in the console uses TanStack Query for caching and refetch; there is no global Redux store — local UI state stays in component state.
DB migrations are forward-only: never edit or delete a shipped migration — add a new one. A change is undone by a follow-up migration, never by rolling back a released one.
New endpoints and behavior changes ship behind a feature flag, defaulted OFF, and are ramped via config — never a bare deploy that flips behavior for everyone at once.
Secrets come only from the secrets manager at runtime — never committed, never in repo env files. A leaked secret is rotated immediately, not just removed from history.

questions.question: What does orchard use for its job queue, and why that choice?
questions.answer: Redis Streams (at-least-once with consumer groups, reusing the Redis they already run).

questions.question: How did Redis Streams compare to SQS on enqueue latency in the queue benchmark?
questions.answer: Redis Streams p99 1.2ms vs SQS 18ms.

questions.question: How does orchard avoid running the same job twice?
questions.answer: A dedup key (sha256 of job_type+payload) in a Redis sorted-set; a re-enqueue within 24h is dropped.

questions.question: What is the retry policy for failed jobs?
questions.answer: Exponential backoff (base 2s, factor 2, cap 5min, max 6 attempts), then dead-letter.

questions.question: What caused the Redis out-of-memory incident and how was it fixed?
questions.answer: The dedup set grew unbounded (per-key TTLs only); fixed with a timestamp-scored sorted-set + periodic sweep.

questions.question: Why were job retries bunching into spikes across workers, and what fixed it?
questions.answer: Jitter used each worker's local clock (clock skew); fixed by jittering off the stored enqueue timestamp.

questions.question: Where does orchard store large job payloads, and what's the size cutoff?
questions.answer: Payloads over 256KB go to S3 (under 256KB inline); over 1MB is rejected with 413.

questions.question: How does orchard keep an always-crashing job from hurting other jobs?
questions.answer: A circuit-breaker dead-letters immediately on a non-retryable error class instead of burning all 6 retries.

questions.question: What metric do orchard workers autoscale on?
questions.answer: Redis Streams queue lag (KEDA), not CPU.

questions.question: How is a job's execution traced back to the request that submitted it?
questions.answer: OpenTelemetry trace context is propagated from the enqueue request into the job's spans.

questions.question: What does orchard's readiness check require?
questions.answer: Redis and Postgres reachable and the worker has claimed its consumer-group slot.

questions.question: Can a dead-lettered job be replayed yet?
questions.answer: The API (POST /dlq/{id}/replay) is done; the console view is still pending.

questions.question: What framework is the console built with?
questions.answer: A React SPA built with Vite, no SSR.

questions.question: How do operators sign in to the console?
questions.answer: OIDC via Okta; the token is held in memory only.

questions.question: What's the rule about changing a DB migration that already shipped?
questions.answer: Never edit a shipped migration — add a new one (forward-only).

questions.question: How are new endpoints rolled out safely?
questions.answer: Behind a feature flag defaulted off, ramped via config.

questions.question: How does the orchard API authenticate requests today?
questions.answer: Short-lived RS256 JWTs (15-min) from the auth service; static API keys were removed.

questions.question: How does orchard run delayed jobs?
questions.answer: A Redis sorted-set scored by run-at + a mover process (no per-job timers).

questions.question: Is the orchard enqueue endpoint rate limited?
questions.answer: Yes — a per-API-key Redis token bucket (1000/min, burst 2000), 429 over limit. (An older note said it was an unlimited TODO.)

questions.question: What message queue does orchard use for real-time websocket fan-out to the console?
questions.answer: REFUSE

questions.question: Which database does orchard use for analytics and reporting dashboards?
questions.answer: REFUSE

questions.question: What is orchard's GDPR right-to-be-forgotten data-deletion process?
questions.answer: REFUSE

questions.question: What is the SLA for orchard job execution latency?
questions.answer: REFUSE

questions.question: How does orchard replicate its job queue across regions for failover?
questions.answer: REFUSE

questions.question: What is the scheduled rotation interval for orchard's JWT signing keys?
questions.answer: REFUSE

questions.question: How does the console support working offline?
questions.answer: REFUSE

questions.question: Is there a native mobile app for the console?
questions.answer: REFUSE
